Alright, your backs, let's dive right in with the fourth and let's by far the most powerful way to lower a prospect's action threshold, which is to use the power of language to cause a very high action threshold prospect to abandon that terribly toxic strategy they have of running unrealistically long negative movies and extremely abbreviated positive ones and getting to embrace the exact opposite strategy instead. And just so you know, this particular pattern works in any industry. It doesn't matter what you're selling, stocks, bonds, insurance, TVs at Best Buy, bikes in a bicycle shop, Mercedes Benz in a gorgeous pearl line dealership, online coaching, digital marketing services. This works for anything and everything both offline and online. Alright, so don't panic if the sample pattern I use now is not a word for word match with your particular industry. Doesn't matter. As soon as you're done here with this lesson and you finish your quiz, I'll take you through a step by step process during your exercises that'll show you how to seamlessly adapt this pattern to your specific industry. Sound good? Alright, great. So here's the deal. Again, I know I'm kind of beating a dead horse here, so I got to beat it just a little bit more, but one very important thing to remember here is that with the exception of your first loop where you actually deflected your prospect's initial objection, all of your subsequent loops are going to start by actually answering whatever new objection your prospect hit you with the second time. And you're going to use one of the dozens of proven rebuttals that I'm handing you in the resource center, albeit with a tacit understanding that no matter how awesome one of my rebuttals might sound, the only thing it's going to do for you here is earn you the right to speak more. You understand that? In other words, it's what you say after the rebuttal that's going to persuade your prospect to close. So now to execute our second loop, remember we have to answer the objection our prospect hit us with. Now remember, this is the second objection now. The first objection we ran our first loop and deflected it. Can't do that now. We have to answer it head on. Now typically you'll find people will hop from one objection to the next. They don't stick with the same one very often. Either way is fine though. But let's say you started by saying, I want to think about it. I said, okay, you ran your loop. I want to think about it. You deflected it and you asked for the order. Now he says, oh wow, it sounds great. Let me just call you back. Well now you have to answer that objection head on. Now there are many different ways to do that. And I'll give you a 20, a 30 in the resource area. Let me choose a common one that works for many situations. But again, you have to make sure the objection you choose fits the context. For example, I would say something along the lines of, Bill, I understand you want to call back, but I've been doing this for quite some time now. If there's one thing I've learned is that call back simply don't work. You're a busy guy. I'm a busy guy. And I don't have the time to follow up. And I know you'll love the idea and I don't want you to miss out. In fact, let me say this. Dot, dot, dot. That dot, dot, dot. Now is your opportunity where you can now loop back once more into presentation mode to the front half of the sale to create more certainty for each of the three tenths. Because remember what big horse certainty wins. We're always doing that. So whenever you run a loop, you're always going to be looping back. That's why they call it a loop, right? You know, essentially go back and button up certainty. You're going to resell the product, resell yourself, resell the company, except in this third pattern now, it's going to be much more based on emotional certainty and future pacing because you already have your airtight logical case locked down tight at this point, right? That's what your first two patterns were all about. So you'll button up a little bit of logical certainty, but now you're really future pacing and you want to get this prospect to a really high level of emotional certainty to make sure you got those first three numbers of his buying combination picked both logically and emotionally. Except this time, rather than transitioning to a close like you did in your first loop, what you're going to do now is go into this magical action threshold lowering pattern. For example, let's say the last few words from that last pattern you just ran that last loop or something along the lines of, and Bill, when you take all those things to get it together, it is just an incredible situation here, Bill. I just made up those words. The point is from those last few words, you're now going to hook on this pattern which starts with this. Bill, let me ask you an honest question here. What's the worst that can possibly happen? Now I'm going to stop for a moment here. What did I just do? I actually just asked Bill the question that's causing him to run the negative leg of his parallel movies. What's the worst that can possibly happen? You get it? See, for someone like me, that question is not a big deal because I'm not going to execute the negative leg of my parallel movie strategy in an overly toxic way. In fact, I'm actually going to blunt it, right? But my dad or Bill, if he's got a high action, such a well-boy, that negative movie, they'll run until they're broke, embarrassed, their wives have divorced them, the neighborhood thinks they're fools, they had to cancel their credit cards, their reputation is destroyed. That's what high action threshold people do. They take the germ of a fear and they blow completely and wildly out of proportion. So when I say to someone like my father or Bill, what's the worst that can possibly happen? Would I stop there? No, because if I did, they'd be like, oh, I'll tell you what could happen and they'll run this wildly long, toxic movie until they're broke, bankrupt, live it under a bridge. You get it? So what I do is I use the power of language, which is the software of the brain, to essentially hijack this toxic strategy they have. So after I say what's the worst that could possibly happen, I actually tell them. For example, in the case of a stock and don't worry, it applies to anything. I'd say, you know, Bill, let's say I'm wrong and the stock drops a few points and you lose a few thousand dollars. I mean, honestly, Bill or Max or Jim Mijel, is that going to put you in the poor house, honestly? To which Max or Bill would say grudgingly, no, wouldn't you say? Exactly. You could destroy your life. And on the upside, let's say I'm right, like we both think I am. And the stock runs up 20 points and you make $20,000. It'll make you feel really good there. It's not going to make you the richest man in town. Now will it? And they'll say, no, that's true too. And I say, exactly, Bill. It's not going to make you rich. It's not going to make you poor. But what this trade will do is serve as a benchmark for future business. It'll show you that I'm the kind of guy that can put you into the market at the right time and also take you out as well. And Bill, by the way, what I could do to you besides this one trade in the way of, da, da, da, whoah, and here's the magic. From here, I'm going to do for Bill what he has never done for himself. Namely, I'm going to have him run a long, fully developed, empowering movie. You get it? What was I thinking when I ran my golf course movie? Remember that? I was on the 18th of August or a pebble beach, whatever it was, right? I just did a perfect four. I went straight to the pin. Everyone was clapping and cheering in the stands, right? That's what people like me do with low action. So we run these great movies. Well, now I'm going to do this for Bill. So I say, you know, what I could do to you besides this one, whatever, this trade, this product sale, this one car, this, whatever the item is, and I will start future pacing, the relationship. I'll let him know, A, how he's going to feel in a few months from now when he's using the product and feeling wonderful. I'll tell him things about how great he's going to have me in his life for the long term, how he can be an asset to him and his family and so forth. You get this now. Here's the deal. We're going to develop the perfect future pacing pattern for your industry in the exercises coming right after this, right? So just remember here, if it doesn't fit, don't worry. It's so easy to develop because here's the deal. They're going to run the movie based on your words. Essentially, you're hijacking in a very positive way. You're hijacking your prospects negative strategy. They've been running this negative disempowering strategy for God only knows how long. I mean, my dad, he went through his life lacking so many things because of this terribly toxic strategy he had of running these artificially pumped up negative moves. It stopped him from buying and getting things he truly needed. You get it? So after your lowest only my dad's action threshold, what do you do next? Well, you're going to transition directly into a close and ask for the order. And also if possible, I would engage in another step down sale, meaning if I can reduce the amount of product they're buying, I would say something along the lines of, you know, Max or Bill, why don't we do this? Since it's our first time working together, why don't we start off a bit small? Instead of picking up a block of a thousand chairs, whatever it was, whatever the product was, a five of these, a hundred of these, right? Let's pick up 500 chairs instead. Now you'll get a cash outlay of less than $5,000 as opposed to $10,000, half as much. Now, obviously you make a bit less money as the stock trades hire or whatever the case is for what you're selling, right? But your percentage gain remains the same and you can judge me on that alone. And believe me, Bill or Max or Jane or John, if you do even one pence as well as the rest of my clients in this program in six months from now and I'll even future pace close, you'll be sitting there feeling this way or that way and you'll be happiest guy in town. Does that sound fair enough? And I shut up and I wait for a response. In other words, if the prospect doesn't quickly answer, do not feel compelled to fill the conversational vacuum. It's not jabbering away and talking through your clothes. Please don't do that. You see, here's the deal. You're at that magic moment right now. We're in perfect sequence. You summed up the very best benefits logically and emotionally. You ran your energy in benefits outpads to reduce energy expenditure, increased benefits. You've lowered the action threshold now and you've asked for the order in just the right way using that tritonal closing pattern. So be quiet and let the prospect answer. If you do this right, you'll find about 75% of them right now who ultimately buy from you will pull the trigger right here after the second loop. You see, by taking these high action threshold buyers and for those few fleeting moments, you'll lower their action thresholds and step through that glorious window. Well, you're about to close what will become the most loyal customers of all. Yet ironically, these ultra-loyal, high lucrative, high action threshold people like my dad, they're the ones who end up slipping through the fingers of virtually all other salespeople except natural bull closers and those who have mastered the straight line system, which now includes you, or close to it. In fact, actually, what's becoming even reasonably proficient at this? These so-called ultra-tough buyers? Will nothing more than routine closes for you that you have to take a bit further down the straight line as a result of their limiting beliefs about buying, which required you to crack that fourth number of the combination, which was to lower their action threshold. You get it? All right, with that, you're in a great spot here. Pretty close to really be able to close anyone's closeable here. Let's take a quiz, lock this stuff down, also go through the exercises very carefully, and as soon as you're done, I will see you in the next lesson.
